query: Are there any bats in Southern China?

Cave deposits show it was once more widely distributed in Fiji (Viti Levu) and Tonga (‘ Eua) but has become extinct since human arrival. Surveys in Fiji suggest its range is quite limited, and it is absent from many apparently suitable areas. Bibliography. treatments.chunk_text: Jamaica. Status and Conservation. Classified as Least Concern on The IUCN Red List. The Buffy Flower Bat is considered common throughoutits restricted distribution. In the Bahamas,it is the most widespread species of bat. In Cuba,it is very common and has been collected in all but one province (Camagtiey). Reports from Jamaica vary, but it appears to be less common than on other islands. It occurs in protected areas. Bibliography. Baker, August & Steuter (1978), Fleming et al. (2009), Genowayset al. treatments.chunk_text: Bat. Status and Conservation. Classified as Least Concern on The IUCN Red List. The Thumbless Bat is listed as least concern in the Ecuadorian Red Book of Endangered Species but vulnerable in the Brazilian Red Book of Endangered Species. The Thumbless Bat was long considered one of the most rare Neotropical bats, butit has been discovered recently in relatively large aggregations. Bibliography. treatments.chunk_text: Family EMBALLONURIDAE (SHEATH-TAILED BATS) • Small- to medium-sized insectivorous bats with tail protruding above uropatagium radio-metacarpal sacs and gular sac often prominent in males. • 3-5-17 cm. • Afrotropical, Indo-Malayan, Australasian, Neotropical, and Holarctic (marginally) Regions. • Closed and open forests to savannas and semi-deserts. • 14 genera, 54 species, 94 taxa. • 1 species Critically Endangered, 2 Endangered, 3 Vulnerable; none Extinct since 1600.

treatments.articleTitle: Eumops perotis (Schinz, 1821) (Chiroptera, Molossidae): a new genus and species for Chile revealed by acoustic surveys
treatments.articleAuthor: Rodríguez-San Pedro, Annia, Pacheco, Jaime A., Beltrán, Clemente, Allendes, Juan Luis & Ugarte-Núñez, Joaquín A.
treatments.chunk_text: Eumops Miller (1906) is a genus of insectivorous bats of the family Molossidae known as bonneted bats or mastiff bats only found in the Americas, occupying a wide variety of habitats from sea level to 3200 m elevation (Velikov 2019). Currently, Eumops includes 17 species, 13 of which have been documented in South America, however, none of them have been previously reported in Chile (DÍaz et al. 2016).

treatments.journalTitle: Handbook of the Mammals of the World – Volume 9 Bats
treatments.chunk_text: Classified as Least Concern on The IUCN Red List. Although not very common, Nathusius’s Pipistrelle is quite widespread. Although it has been more common in northern countries,it is now more frequently reported in the south, and during the last few decades, it has been reported or established in the Netherlands, UK, Switzerland, Italy, and Spain. Because it is one of the few long-distance migratory bat species in Europe, modification or fragmentation of habitat and commuting routes might compromise its movements. Roost disturbance and destruction, loss of mature trees in wetlands, and direct persecution are recognized as direct threats. Bibliography. Arslan & Zima (2014), Benda, Georgiakakis et al. response.answer:
Yes, there are several species of bats found in Southern China. Here are a few examples:

1. Chinese Pipistrelle (Pipistrellus subflavus): This is one of the most common bat species in China and can be found throughout Southern China.
2. Chinese Leaf-nosed Bat (Rhinolophus affinis): This species is also widely distributed in Southern China and is known for its distinctive leaf-shaped nose.
3. Indian Flying Fox (Pteropus giganteus): Although not exclusive to Southern China, this large fruit bat can be found in the region's tropical forests.
4. Chinese Fruit Bat (Dobsonia sinensis): This species is endemic to Southern China and is found in the country's subtropical forests.

According to the IUCN Red List, several bat species are listed as Endangered or Vulnerable in Southern China due to habitat loss, climate change, and other human activities. Some examples include:

* Chinese Horseshoe Bat (Rhinolophus affinis): Listed as Endangered on the IUCN Red List.
* Chinese Yellow Bat (Eonycteris spelaea): Listed as Vulnerable on the IUCN Red List.

It's worth noting that bat diversity can vary greatly depending on the region, habitat, and time of year. If you're interested in learning more about bats in Southern China or would like to know more about specific species, I'd be happy to help!
